The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=10, col=31).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 45, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking the red cross area revealed many red pixels! Red pixels appear at the "missing" pixel positions inside the source blocks (N block has 14/15 pixels, Y bar has 14/15 pixels). The red pixels mark where the blocks are incomplete. Goal hypothesis: Click on the red pixels inside the source blocks to fill them completely (15 pixels each).
- Plan: Click on the red pixel at (10, 31) inside the N block to fill it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 44 actions; a strong score needs about 20 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the N block clearly:
```
  9: BBGNNNNNBB
  10: BBGNNNrNBB
  11: BBGNNNNNBB
```
So the N block is a 3x5 rectangle (rows 9-11, cols 28-32) with the center pixel at (10, 31) being red (r). The G bar is at col 27, rows 9-11.
And the Y block:
```
  27: BBGGGBB
  28: BBYrYBB
  29: BBYYYBB
```
So the Y block is a 2x3 rectangle (rows 28-29, cols 9-11) with the pixel at (28, 10) being red (r). The G bar is at row 27, cols 9-11.
Wait, I notice that the G bar above the Y block has 3 pixels (cols 9-11), which matches the width of the Y block. And the G bar to the left of the N block has 3 pixels (rows 9-11), which matches the height of the N block.
So the G bars seem to be indicators of the source block dimensions, not sizes.
Now, let me think about what the goal might be. The source blocks have red pixels marking the "missing" pixel positions. Maybe the goal is to remove the red pixels by making the source blocks complete.
But clicking on the red pixels doesn't seem to work. Maybe I need to click on the G bars to fill the source blocks?
Actually, wait. Let me look at the G bars more carefully. The G bar to the left of the N block is at col 27, rows 9-11. The G bar above the Y block is at row 27, cols 9-11. These G bars are 3 pixels each.
But the N block is 3x5=15 pixels with 1 missing (14 pixels), and the Y block is 2x3=6 pixels with 1 missing (5 pixels). So the G bars don't match the source block sizes.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The G bars might be "fill" buttons. When I click on the G bar, it fills the source block. Let me try clicking on the G bar to the left of the N block at (10, 27).
Wait, I already tried clicking on (10, 27), and it only changed the timer. But maybe I need to click on a different pixel of the G bar, or maybe the G bars only work when the source blocks are at a specific size.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to click on the red pixels in a specific order or pattern. Let me try clicking on all the red pixels in sequence.
Actually wait, I've been spending too many actions without making progress. Let me step back and think about what I know:
1. The initial state was N=14, Y=23.
2. Clicking on the U-shapes toggles the source blocks between different sizes.
3. The red pixels mark the "missing" pixel positions inside the source blocks.
4. Clicking on the red pixels only changes the timer.
5. Clicking on the G bars only changes the timer.
6. Clicking on the red crosses only changes the timer.
Maybe the goal is to have the source blocks at specific sizes. The initial state was N=14, Y=23. Maybe the goal is to have N=14, Y=23, which is the initial state. But that doesn't make sense, because the level would already be complete at the start.
Actually, wait. Let me check if the level was already complete at the start by looking at the initial state's done flag.
Actually, I don't have access to the initial state's done flag. But I know that the level is not complete at the current state (N=14, Y=5).
Let me try a different hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to have the source blocks at sizes where the red pixels disappear. But the red pixels always seem to be present, regardless of the source block size.
Actually, let me check if the red pixels disappear when the source blocks are at certain sizes. Let me toggle the N block to 5 and check if the red pixel disappears.
Wait, actually I just realized something. The N block at 14 pixels has the red pixel at (10, 31), which is the center pixel. The N block at 5 pixels has the red pixel at (10, 28), which is also the center pixel (but of a smaller rectangle). So the red pixel always marks the center pixel of the source block.
Maybe the goal is to have the source blocks at sizes where the center pixel is not missing, i.e., the source blocks are complete. But the source blocks always have the center pixel missing, regardless of their size.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the N block at 14 pixels:
```
  9: BBGNNNNNBB
  10: BBGNNNrNBB
  11: BBGNNNNNBB
```
The N block is a 3x5 rectangle with the center pixel at (10, 31) being red. So the N block has 14 N pixels and 1 r pixel.
And the N block at 5 pixels:
```
  9: BBGNNBB...
  10: BBGNNBB...
  11: BBGNNBB...
```
Wait, I need to check the ascii for the N block at 5 pixels. Let me toggle the N block to 5 and check.
Actually, I already know the N block at 5 pixels is at rows 9-11, cols 28-29. So it's a 3x2 rectangle. The red pixel is at (10, 28), which is the center pixel of the 3x2 rectangle.
Hmm, so the source blocks always have the center pixel missing, regardless of their size. This means the goal is not to have complete source blocks.
Let me try yet another hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to match the source blocks to the U-shapes in the containers. The N container has two N U-shapes, and the Y container has two Y U-shapes. Maybe the goal is to have the source blocks at sizes that match the number of U-shapes multiplied by some factor.
Actually, I'm running out of ideas. Let me try clicking on the c (charcoal) regions inside the containers. These are the dark gray regions inside the N and Y containers.
The c regions are:
- c id=10: pixels=18, rows=19-23, cols=37-41 - left c region inside the N container
- c id=12: pixels=18, rows=19-23, cols=43-47 - right c region inside the N container
- c id=19: pixels=18, rows=36-40, cols=22-26 - top c region inside the Y container
- c id=22: pixels=18, rows=42-46, cols=22-26 - bottom c region inside the Y container
Let me try clicking on the c regions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the c region inside the N container (left side)
# c id=10: pixels=18, rows=19-23, cols=37-41
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 39}])
print("After clicking c region at (21, 39):")
print("Result:", last_action_result)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'Y'] and n['pixels'] != 7:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
